content.intro: Before writing a single query, let's understand what we're actually trying to do.

blocks.content: Data analytics is the practice of examining raw data to find patterns, draw conclusions, and support decisions. It is not about making charts. It is about answering questions with evidence.
Every analytics question starts the same way: someone needs to make a decision, and they don't have enough information to make it confidently.
An analyst's job is to close that gap — to take messy, incomplete data and turn it into clear, honest answers.
There are four types of analytics questions, and they build on each other:

items.label: Descriptive
items.detail: What happened? (Sales were down 12% last quarter.)

items.label: Diagnostic
items.detail: Why did it happen? (The drop was concentrated in the North region.)

items.label: Predictive
items.detail: What is likely to happen? (If the trend continues, we'll miss target by Q3.)

items.label: Prescriptive
items.detail: What should we do? (Increase field sales activity in the North by 20%.)

blocks.content: Most people stop at descriptive. The real value of analytics is in diagnostic and predictive work — asking why and what next.

blocks.question: A marketing team asks: 'Which campaign brought in the most customers last month?' What type of analytics question is this?
options: Prescriptive — it tells the team what to do
blocks.explanation: Descriptive analytics answers 'what happened' questions. Asking which campaign performed best is looking backward at a result — that's descriptive.
